walkthrough.title: Unbox and Sort the Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's get What Do You Meme set up! Open the box and you will find two types of cards. The larger cards with funny photos on them are the photo cards. The smaller cards with text on them are the caption cards. Separate them into two decks.

walkthrough.title: Shuffle Both Decks
walkthrough.text: Give both the photo card deck and the caption card deck a thorough shuffle. These decks are big, so feel free to split them in half and riffle shuffle each half. Place both shuffled decks face down in the center of the table where everyone can reach them.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Easel
walkthrough.text: Place the easel or card stand where all players can see it. This is where the current round's photo card will be displayed. Make sure nobody has to crane their neck to see the image. Good sight lines make the game funnier because everyone reacts to the photo at the same time.

walkthrough.title: Deal Caption Cards
walkthrough.text: Deal seven caption cards face down to each player. Go ahead and look at your hand but keep it hidden from other players. These are your tools for creating the funniest meme combinations. You will draw back to seven after each round.

walkthrough.title: Choose the First Judge
walkthrough.text: Pick someone to be the first judge using any method you like. The judge role rotates clockwise each round, so everyone will get a turn. The judge does not submit a caption card during their round. Instead, they reveal the photo and pick the winner.

summary.title: Unbox and Sort the Cards
bullets: Separate photo cards (large, with images) from caption cards (small, with text)
75 photo cards + 435 caption cards in the base game

summary.title: Shuffle Both Decks
bullets: Shuffle photo deck + caption deck separately
Place both face-down centrally

summary.title: Set Up the Easel
bullets: Place easel/stand where all players can see
Photo card will be displayed here each round

summary.title: Deal Caption Cards
bullets: Deal 7 caption cards to each player
Keep hand hidden from others
Draw back to 7 after each round

summary.title: Choose the First Judge
bullets: Any method to select first judge
Judge rotates clockwise each round
Judge reveals photo + picks winner (does not submit)

walkthrough.title: The Judge Reveals a Photo
walkthrough.text: Each round begins with the judge drawing the top photo card from the deck and placing it on the easel for everyone to see. Take a moment to look at the image and let the laughter settle. This photo is what every player will pair a caption with to create the funniest meme.

walkthrough.title: Players Submit Captions
walkthrough.text: Every player except the judge looks at their hand of seven caption cards and picks the one they think makes the funniest combination with the photo. Place your chosen caption card face down in the center of the table. Keep it anonymous so the judge does not know who submitted which card.

walkthrough.title: The Judge Reads Them Aloud
walkthrough.text: Once all captions are submitted, the judge collects and shuffles them so no one knows the order. Then the judge reads each caption card aloud while holding it next to the photo. A dramatic or funny reading makes the game much better, so judges should ham it up.

walkthrough.title: The Judge Picks a Winner
walkthrough.text: After reading all the captions, the judge picks their favorite combination. This is entirely subjective. There is no correct answer and no appeals process. The judge's decision is final. The winning player reveals themselves and takes the photo card as their point.

walkthrough.title: Draw Back Up
walkthrough.text: All players who submitted a caption draw one new caption card from the deck to get back to seven cards in hand. The non-winning captions go to a discard pile. If the caption deck ever runs out, shuffle the discard pile to form a new deck.

walkthrough.title: Rotate the Judge
walkthrough.text: The player to the left of the current judge becomes the new judge. They draw a new photo card and the next round begins. The previous judge rejoins as a regular player and can submit captions again. Play continues clockwise through the group.

walkthrough.title: Winning the Game
walkthrough.text: Before you start, agree on how many photo cards a player needs to win. The most common target is five or seven points. Alternatively, you can play until the photo deck runs out or for a set number of rounds. The player with the most photo cards at the end wins.

Most photo cards = winner

walkthrough.title: Welcome to What Do You Meme
walkthrough.text: Welcome to your first game of What Do You Meme! We will walk through a practice round with six players. One player will be the judge and the other five will submit captions. Let's see how a round plays out from start to finish.

walkthrough.title: The Judge Reveals the Photo
walkthrough.text: Our judge, Alex, draws the top photo card and places it on the easel. The photo shows a dog staring blankly at a computer screen. Everyone looks at the image and starts laughing. Now each of the five other players needs to pick the perfect caption from their hand.

walkthrough.title: Players Choose Their Captions
walkthrough.text: Each player scans their seven caption cards for the funniest match. Beth picks "When you pretend to understand the meeting." Carlos picks "Me doing my taxes." Dana picks "When the WiFi goes out." Everyone places their cards face down in the center.

walkthrough.title: Alex Reads the Submissions
walkthrough.text: Alex shuffles the five submitted captions and reads them one by one while holding each next to the dog photo. The room erupts when Alex reads "When you pretend to understand the meeting" with a deadpan delivery. Several captions get big laughs.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Playing Your Favorite
walkthrough.text: Carlos submitted "Me doing my taxes" because it made him laugh the hardest. But Alex has a dry sense of humor and picks absurdist comedy over relatable humor. Carlos played what was funniest to himself instead of what would appeal to the judge. This is the most common beginner mistake.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Play to the Judge
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in What Do You Meme is reading the judge. Pay attention to what makes them laugh during the reading. Do they prefer absurd non-sequiturs, dark humor, or wholesome comedy? Your caption should target the judge's taste, not your own.

walkthrough.title: Alex Picks a Winner
walkthrough.text: Alex picks "When you pretend to understand the meeting" as the winner. Beth reveals it was her card and takes the photo card as her first point. The non-winning captions go to the discard pile, and everyone draws back up to seven cards.

walkthrough.title: Round Two: New Judge
walkthrough.text: Beth becomes the new judge. She draws a photo of a cat sitting in a tiny box that is way too small for it. The players submit their captions. This time, Carlos thinks about what Beth finds funny instead of what he finds funny. He picks a relatable caption about online shopping.

walkthrough.title: Beginner Mistake: Ignoring the Photo
walkthrough.text: Eric submitted a generically funny caption that does not connect to the cat-in-a-box photo at all. It says "That face when your alarm goes off." It is funny on its own but has nothing to do with the image. Beth does not pick it because the best memes have a specific connection between the photo and the caption.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Connect Caption to Photo
walkthrough.text: The funniest memes pair a caption that specifically relates to what is happening in the photo. A generic funny caption will almost always lose to a caption that directly references the image. Look at the photo closely and ask yourself what story it tells before choosing your card.

walkthrough.title: The Game Continues
walkthrough.text: The group plays several more rounds. The judge rotates clockwise, and players learn each other's humor styles. By round five, everyone is playing to the current judge instead of to themselves. The laughter keeps building as players find better and better combinations.

walkthrough.title: Congratulations
walkthrough.text: Congratulations! You have seen how What Do You Meme works from setup through multiple rounds. The key lessons are to play to the judge's sense of humor and to connect your caption specifically to the photo. Those two skills will win you more rounds than any other strategy.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Meme
walkthrough.text:
Before you start your real game, here are three things to remember.

First, check out General Tips for strategies on reading judges and managing your hand.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick rundown of all the rules and variants.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

summary.title: Ready to Meme
bullets: Check out General Tips → reading judges and hand management
Reference the Appendix → rules and variants
Ask your personal GM Guide → tap Q&A for instant answers during play

walkthrough.title: Read the Judge
walkthrough.text: The single most important skill in this game is understanding what makes the current judge laugh. Watch their reactions as they read submissions each round. Some judges prefer dark humor, others go for wholesome comedy, and some love absurd nonsense. Adapt your submissions to fit the judge's taste every single round.

walkthrough.title: Connect Caption to Photo
walkthrough.text: The best memes have a specific, obvious connection between the caption and the image. A generically funny caption will almost always lose to one that directly references what is happening in the photo. Before picking your card, ask yourself: does this caption tell a story about this specific image?

walkthrough.title: Do Not Hoard Good Captions
walkthrough.text: New players often hold onto their best caption cards waiting for the perfect photo. The problem is that perfect match may never come. Play your strongest card each round while it is still relevant. You will draw a new card every round, so your hand is constantly refreshing.

walkthrough.title: Burn Weak Cards Strategically
walkthrough.text: When the judge's humor style does not match your hand at all, use the round to dump your weakest caption card. You are unlikely to win anyway, so cycle out a bad card and draw a fresh one. This improves your hand for the next round when the judge might be a better fit.

walkthrough.title: Deliver with Flair as Judge
walkthrough.text: When you are the judge, the way you read captions dramatically affects how funny they land. Give each caption a proper dramatic reading. Pause for effect. Change your voice. Sell the joke. A flat monotone reading can kill even the funniest caption, and an enthusiastic delivery can make a mediocre one shine.

walkthrough.title: Go Specific Over Generic
walkthrough.text: A caption that says something very specific about the photo will almost always beat a caption that is funny in a general way. If the photo shows someone with a shocked face, a caption like "When you check your bank account after vacation" beats "That feeling when something happens" every time.

walkthrough.title: Trust Your First Laugh
walkthrough.text: When you look at the photo and your captions, the first card that makes you laugh is usually your best pick. Overthinking leads to clever-but-unfunny choices. Comedy is instinctive. If a caption makes you snort when you first see it paired with the photo, it will probably make the judge laugh too.

summary.title: Read the Judge
bullets: Watch judge reactions every round
Adapt to their humor: dark, wholesome, absurd, etc.
Each new judge = new strategy

summary.title: Connect Caption to Photo
bullets: Specific photo connection > generic funny
Ask: does this caption tell a story about THIS image?

summary.title: Do Not Hoard Good Captions
bullets: Play strongest card each round
Perfect match may never come
Hand refreshes every round anyway

summary.title: Burn Weak Cards Strategically
bullets: Bad hand + mismatched judge = dump weakest card
Cycle bad cards → draw fresh for next round

summary.title: Deliver with Flair as Judge
bullets: Dramatic reading sells the joke
Pause for effect, change voice
Flat delivery kills even the funniest caption

summary.title: Go Specific Over Generic
bullets: Specific captions beat broadly funny ones
Reference what's actually in the photo

summary.title: Trust Your First Laugh
bullets: First card that makes you laugh = usually best pick
Overthinking → clever but unfunny
Comedy is instinctive — go with your gut

walkthrough.title: Judge Profiling
walkthrough.text: Over multiple rounds, build a mental profile of each player as a judge. Track which captions they pick and what humor style wins. When that player becomes judge again, you already know their preferences. This long-term tracking is the biggest edge in a full game.

walkthrough.title: The Setup Caption
walkthrough.text: Sometimes the funniest caption is not the one with the punchline. It is the one that makes the judge re-read it and slowly realize why it is funny. These slow-burn captions work best with thoughtful judges who take their time picking a winner rather than going with the first big laugh.

walkthrough.title: Misdirection for Laughs
walkthrough.text: A caption that starts in one direction and goes somewhere unexpected is a classic comedy technique. If the photo shows a grandma, a caption that begins sweetly and takes a dark turn can catch the judge off guard. Subverted expectations generate some of the biggest laughs in the game.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Room Temperature
walkthrough.text: Pay attention to the group's energy level. Early in the game, people are warmed up and receptive to edgier humor. Later in the evening, as people get tired or drinks have flowed, simpler and more relatable captions tend to win. Match your submission intensity to the room's current mood.

walkthrough.title: The Callback Play
walkthrough.text: If a previous round had a particularly memorable photo-caption combo, look for opportunities to submit a caption that references it. Callbacks to shared jokes create an inside-joke dynamic that judges love. This works especially well in longer games where the group builds shared references.

walkthrough.title: Competitive Hand Management
walkthrough.text: In a serious competitive game, track which types of captions you still have in your hand. If you are heavy on dark humor but the next three judges prefer clean comedy, you need to cycle those cards out. Burn one dark caption per round to draw fresh cards that better match the upcoming judges.

walkthrough.title: Judging as Kingmaking
walkthrough.text: As judge, you have subtle power. If two captions are equally funny but one belongs to the player in last place, picking the underdog keeps the game competitive. Conversely, if the leader is about to win, you might subconsciously favor other players. Be aware of this bias and decide how you want to use it.

summary.title: Judge Profiling
bullets: Track each player's judging preferences across rounds
Apply profiles when they judge again
Biggest competitive edge in the game

summary.title: The Setup Caption
bullets: Slow-burn captions that reward a re-read
Works with thoughtful, deliberate judges

summary.title: Misdirection for Laughs
bullets: Captions that subvert expectations
Start sweet → dark twist, or vice versa
Surprise generates biggest laughs

summary.title: Reading the Room Temperature
bullets: Early game → edgier humor works
Late game / tired group → simpler, relatable wins
Match intensity to group energy

summary.title: The Callback Play
bullets: Reference a memorable earlier combo
Inside-joke callbacks → judges love them
Better in longer sessions with shared references

summary.title: Competitive Hand Management
bullets: Track what caption types you hold
Burn mismatched cards to improve hand for upcoming judges
1 bad-match burn per round → better draws

summary.title: Judging as Kingmaking
bullets: Judge has subtle power to keep game competitive
Equally funny → favor underdog to tighten the race

entries.definition: A large card featuring a meme-worthy image. One is revealed each round and paired with caption cards. Won photo cards serve as points.
A standard-size card with humorous text. Players select one from their hand each round to pair with the current photo card.
A card stand used to display the current round's photo card where all players can see it.
The player who reveals the photo card and selects the winning caption each round. Rotates clockwise. Does not submit a caption during their judging round.
A player's collection of seven caption cards, kept hidden from other players. Replenished to seven after each round by drawing from the caption deck.
The act of placing a chosen caption card face-down in the center of the table during a round. Submissions are anonymous until the judge reads them.
Earned by winning a round. The winning player takes the photo card. Each photo card equals one point toward the win target.
Where non-winning caption cards go after each round. Reshuffled into a new draw deck if the caption deck runs out.
The agreed-upon number of photo cards needed to win the game. Commonly set at five to seven points before the game begins.
A house rule that adds a sixty-second timer for caption submissions to keep the game moving faster. Not in the official rules but widely used.
A house rule where all players vote on the funniest caption instead of a single judge deciding. The judge breaks ties.
Additional themed sets of photo and caption cards that shuffle directly into the base game decks. Many themes are available.
